sentences.text: He said to my mother, Esther, why don't you make more bread, and Danish, whatever.
He says, because tomorrow they starting to take us away.
[PAUSES] And we didn't go to sleep that night, my mother and I.
I said, well, they call him [NON-ENGLISH], they call him the crazy German.
But I don't think, I said to my mother, that he'll ever get to us.
Yes.
Yes.
The next day, they came, and they told us that we are allowed to take like 50 kilos of food and-- and clothing with us.
And that we'll be relocated somewhere in Poland.
Yes.
Yeah.
We took some pictures.
We had an album, and we took some pictures.
No.
Yes.
They took us to a-- to the-- in a synagogue yard, and then to the train.
Yes.
Yes, all the Jews.
And they took us on a train, and they took us to a ghetto.
No, cattle train.
Well, there were no seats, nothing.
We're just sitting on the floor.
And-- and my mother was the happiest.
And so I was the happiest person, too.
And I remember her taking-- hugging me, and saying to me, I am so happy that we are together.
Whatever's going to happen to us, at least we'll-- we'll be together.
And I said, I'm happy, too.
I'll never regret it.
And I told her, I said, you know, I asked this friend, Esther, who lived across [? from the ?] street.
I said, aren't you coming home to be with your family?
She says, no.
I don't want to go home.
I'm gonna stay here.
Believe it or not, she was taken the next few days from-- in Budapest.
And they took her to Auschwitz.

And there, of all-- all the things, she was put to work in the crematorium to take care of the clothes that they told the people who came to the crematorium to undress and just make sure they're on the same hook.
And you're just going to take a shower, and then you'll be back.
And you'll have your clothes.
Yeah, she came across the clothes that she would send home to her family.
[SIGHS]
It started on a Sunday morning was the last holiday we were together.
It was Shavuot.
And [PAUSES] we arrived there on Tuesday morning.
We didn't know where, but we knew that this--
No.
They just put in a few barrels, and people would-- you know, there was no bathroom, nothing.
Oh, yeah, there were like [? a ?] 20 people who died on the train, till we got to Auschwitz.
Yes, they came up and finally and take them out.
We don't know what happened, how they exposed of them.
It was rough-- older people were dying, younger kids were screaming.
We were in Slovakia, just crossing the border.
And there until then, the Hungarians would be on the train with us.
They stepped up and the train was leaving.
[PAUSES] And then the Germans took over.
No.
Nothing, just fields, little towns.
Pardon me?
Oh, around 2 o'clock.
In the afternoon, yeah.
We-- we couldn't do nothing.
We-- they just told us to behave, not to be, you-- you know, mischievous.
And we just tried to listen.
Because we didn't know what the outcome of all this will be.
They took us to Auschwitz.
I remember when we went to Auschwitz.
They-- they made a last stop in Cracow.
And they put in a few pails of water for us.
And they-- all they were doing is warning us not to carry on.
Just be as calm as you can.
Because soon you will reach your destination.
Yes.
We-- we left like Sunday morning.
And we arrived Tuesday morning.
Yes.
Well, they just jumped up on the train.
They opened the doors.
And they told us to get out as fast as we can.
We should jump.
We should do anything.
Just get off that train.
And, in a way, we were very glad to get a little fresh air.
[PAUSES] And we got out of the train.
And right away, they screamed, man and-- and younger children should go to the left.
And the mothers and children should go to the right.
And all of a sudden the sort-- the sorting started.
And he came over, and he screamed.
He yelled at us.
That we--
One of the German soldiers.
I-- we didn't jump fast enough.
But there-- there was a-- a humongous group of people.
They couldn't even walk.
They-- we just-- you know, they were pushing one another.
And all of a sudden I see my mother-- oh, I saw him go in the front, the German who was doing the-- the sorting.
And [PAUSES] as he was going up front, he ran back to me.
Because he noticed that I ran back to my mother.
And he beat me up, and he pushed me.
He stabbed me with a bayonet here.
[PAUSES] [SIGHS] I was bleeding because he'd stabbed me with a bayonet here on the right arm.
And all of a sudden, I lifted up my head.
And I saw my mother walking away with the two youngest brothers.
And that was it.
They took us to take a shower.
They shaved us all over.
I was hoping to.
But I never seen her again.
No, because I started bleeding.
And I couldn't-- you know, the blood was just flowing.
And I--
No.
My father I didn't see.
Never.
So they took you for a shower.
For a shower.
They wanted us right away to take off our clothes because we're going to take a shower.
And-- but they never brought us back to same place.
So whatever-- there were shoes, there were clothes on the floor.
So whatever we thought would fit us, we took it, and that's it.
I-- I wore a dress without being washed for-- for about two months.
So that's how I got finally typhus.
Yes.
Yes, only-- yeah, and the men were shaving us all over, our heads.
Wherever we had hair, we were shaving us.
Yes.
It was warm.
Well, they told us to go, and they took us in the camp, in Birkenau.
I didn't know then Birkenau.
But I know now Birkenau.
And [PAUSES] [SIGHS] they just made a-- like a-- what do you call it?
Night-- [PAUSES] instead of the sun, what is it at night, at--
Moon.
No.
They just brought a big hunks like-- like garbage can, and they had so-called soup in it.
But it wasn't a soup.
It was anything but soup.
But anything was good, because we were all dried out on the trip.
They wouldn't give us enough water, even, on the train.
They brought us to Birkenau.
I didn't know it then.
But I know it now.
Yes.
It was evening.
They took us to a-- a barrack.
But there was nothing in that barrack, just mud.
And we couldn't care less.
And we slept in the mud.
And then a few days later, I found out that my two aunts are in a different barrack.
And I was able to escape and to be together with them.
Yes.
Not in all.
The-- the one we were taken was the barrack, 30, 31st barrack in Birkenau.
And it was nothing but mud.
There were no bunk beds in it yet.
Yes.
Like 10.
This was in May.
Yes.
Rough.
They finally were assigning people to work.
And me, they selected to clean, with two other girls, the toilets and pull a horse wagon in the fields, dump it, and [? it ?] come back.
This was our-- our assignment to do.
[SIGHS]
Oh, they used to wake us up like 4:00, 4:00, 4:30 in the morning to count us, like we were able to escape.
Yes.
In the morning and in the evening, too, when they would come.
Whoever went out of the camp to work.
It was about an hour.
Yeah.
Also some so-called coffee, but it was-- anything was good.
Yes.
Yeah.
No.
That meant crematorium.
No.
Oh, yeah.
Well, we had to stay, I think, four or five in a row, I forgot.
[PAUSES] And they would just walk, you know.
And whichever face they didn't like, they would-- they'd pull them out.
Then we were there already a few days, so more or less, especially the Polish Jews, they were there longer than we do.
And they were our biggest enemies.
Because we were home till now.
And they were there already.
I says, but I didn't kill your mother or father.
I-- I had the same thing happen to me just a few days ago.
They were very nasty to us, the newcomers, the-- the Polish girls.
Yes, I did.
I had it taken out.
Oh, because I kind of landed a good job in Miami Beach.
I don't know.
Have you ever heard of the Famous Restaurant?
Yeah?
I worked there 28 years.
Only I would watch over myself.
Be-- be-- there was no such thing who watched over me.
If you were unable to do it for yourself, they would release you right away from the lines and take you right away to the crematoriums.
Because they enlightened us, the Polish girls.
You think this is bad?
We had it worse.
You have it good now.
Oh, yeah.
It-- it was just their [? fun ?] time.
[PAUSES] There was a guard in-- a-- a Gestapo in Auschwitz and Birkenau.
His name was Bogar.
He always walked around with a cane.
And he-- he-- he would say, hey Jew, come on.
I want to tell you something.
And that man right away, because he walked around with a cane, he-- he broke his cane we don't know how many times.
Because that's how we-- he would beat up the Jews.
For no reason.
No, not by him.
The one I really, really despised, and I do till this day, is the one who took me away from my mother.
And he was at a trial in-- in Frankfurt.
And I didn't want to-- I never-- I promised myself I'll never set foot in Germany in my life.
But being bothered, being called from the judge at Frankfurt of the Auschwitz trial, [PAUSES] I decided finally to go.
What would we talk-- how long can we live like this?
How long do you think we'll be alive like this?
And chances were very slim of surviving.
I came once very close.
We got out to a-- for a [GERMAN].
And there were two sisters.
[PAUSES]
And one came over, and she pulled me away.
She says, look, this is my sister.
Why don't you go in-- in the other line?
Because I want us to be together.
Well, they weren't put together for long.
Did you see any children in Birkenau?
Towards the end, yes.
I was there till December 16.
And they also took us out of lines.
And they took us-- did you see, Birkenau on the other side was also a camp.
They took us into that camp.
And then in the morning, they put us on a train.
Yes.
We were working in Stuttgart in ammunition factory.
And there were civilians too who worked there, civilian Germans.
And as we're walking up the stairs, she would always look like this and that and this way.
And she took out from her bag a piece of bread.
And she says, now eat it.
Don't let-- don't-- don't let anybody take this away.
She would do this to me every day.
No.
Because we weren't allowed to mingle.
Why get them in trouble or ourselves?
Yes.
Well, they were very sheltered.
And the ones who were working outside of the camp were tried to bring in, you know, something for-- for the baby to-- to eat.
Because it was-- it was just born there.
No.
Would what?
Oh, yes.
Yes.
[SIGHS]
Yes.
We were.
One would tell the other, you know, that such and such holiday is coming, which were the High Holy Days, like we're going to have now.
[PAUSES] But, yes, they even allowed us to participate.
A lot of us didn't want to eat on that day.
Because we never ate on Yom Kippur.
And-- but some-- this is-- this is how it went on, day after day.
First I wore the dress they gave me, the one I was photographed in.
But later on, we were able to get something from the people who worked in the-- they called that the Kanada.
I'm sure you heard of that.
Shoes, when I arrived there-- I'd left my good shoes in that barrack we took showers.
Never saw those shoes again.
And I-- I happened to get two shoes one was still-- both were-- you know, the different-- one was left, and one was-- I mean one was the right, and this was the left.
And I walked in those Holland wooden shoes.
It was hectic.
But when they took us to that-- in Birkenau-- to that-- where they would give us decent clothes.
We could pick up from the-- whatever we wanted to, and shoes.
And they put us on a train.
So we ended up in Stuttgart.
And then--
Yes.
I would say about five hours.
Yes.
Oh, there was a-- a German guard, a-- a lady.
But she saw-- as she was walking to see from car to car how we are doing.
And I was burning up with-- with fever.
I was delirious.
So I heard she's calling there a name.
And what she was calling was Rachel.
And [PAUSES] she said to her, do you have some aspirins left, the German guard.
She says, yes, I have some.
Why don't you give me-- I want to help out this girl there.
She's laying.
She's screaming.
She's so delirious.
Give me some aspirins.
So she sat down near me, that German guard.
And she was humming.
She was singing a song to herself.
And then Rachel came over and gave her aspirins.
So she was feeding me with aspirins.
Do you remember the song she was humming?
Yes.
[SINGING IN GERMAN] And I-- I remember I was delirious.
And I-- and I remember that song, and I still do.
Yes.
She says that in my homeland the roses are blooming.
And how she wants to go back to-- to that home.
And I'll never forget that song.
So what happened-- can I jump?
Or you have something-- you want to ask me more?
What Stuttgart like-- was like, somehow-- and this is really the way I-- I say it.
I was-- I never felt-- I mean I've seen people just conk out on the floor and that's it.
I never seen dead people, you know, like I saw it in-- in Birkenau.
So anything that was better than Birkenau.
And we finally arrived at our destination.
By then I was feeling much better from the aspirins.
[PAUSES] And she came over.
And she wiped me off, you know, with a-- a wet handkerchief.
And I opened my eyes, and I says, you know, we are not two of a kind.
I says, but I'll never forget you as long as I'll live.
[PAUSES] And she says, I-- I'll hold you to it.
I says, if I'll survive, I says, you can hold me to it.
But destiny somehow always appeared.
And I wasn't destined to-- to die.
